message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Initial cases of COVID-19 reported in Hong Kong were mostly imported from China. However, most cases reported in February 2020 were locally-acquired infections, indicating local community transmissions. We extracted the demographic, clinical and epidemiological data from 50 COVID-19 patients, who accounted for 53.8% of the cases in Hong Kong by February 2020. Whole-genome sequencing of the SARS-CoV-2 were conducted to determine the phylogenetic relatedness and transmission dynamics. Only three (6.0%) patients required ICU admission. Phylogenetic analysis identified six transmission clusters. All locally-acquired cases harboured a common mutation
                  <jats:italic>Orf3a</jats:italic>
                  G251V and were clustered in two subclades in global phylogeny of SARS-CoV-2. The estimated time to the most recent common ancestor of local COVID-2019 outbreak was December 24, 2019 with an evolutionary rate of 3.04×10
                  <jats:sup>−3</jats:sup>
                  substitutions per site per year. The reproduction number value was 1.84. Social distancing and vigilant epidemiological control are crucial to the containment of COVID-19 transmission.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article summary lines</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A combined epidemiological and phylogenetic analysis of early COVID-19 outbreak in Hong Kong revealed that a SARS-CoV-2 variant with
                    <jats:italic>ORF3a</jats:italic>
                    G251V mutation accounted for all locally acquired cases, and that asymptomatic carriers could be a huge public health risk for COVID-19 control.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A territory-wide study of early COVID-19 outbreak in Hong Kong community: A clinical, epidemiological and phylogenomic investigation
